description:
You are helping a bio-informatics researcher quickly review data-ingestion problems that happened on 12 April 2023.  
    Three raw log files are already present:

      • /home/user/research_logs/experiment_A.log  
      • /home/user/research_logs/experiment_B.log  
      • /home/user/research_logs/experiment_control.log  

    1. For experiment_A.log and experiment_B.log pull out only the lines that  
          a. come from the “DataLoad” module, **and**  
          b. have the log-level “WARN” or “ERROR”, **and**  
          c. have a timestamp on 2023-04-12 from 10:00:00 through 11:59:59 (24-h clock, inclusive).  

    2. From experiment_control.log pull out only the lines whose message contains the substring “checksum validated” (case-sensitive, single space between the words).  
       Do **not** apply any time or log-level restriction to this file.

    3. Merge the two result sets (from steps 1 and 2) and sort them strictly chronologically by their time-stamp so the earliest line appears first.

    4. Write the final ordered list to  
          /home/user/analysis/alert_digest_20230412.log  
       The file must contain **only** the nine qualifying log lines, one per line, with the original text preserved exactly (including the surrounding square brackets).  
       No extra blank lines are allowed.

    5. Also create a tiny counter file  
          /home/user/analysis/alert_digest_20230412.count  
       Its contents must be a single line containing two unsigned integers separated by a single comma with **no** spaces, followed by a trailing newline:  
          <number_of_WARN_or_ERROR_lines>,<number_of_checksum_lines>\n  

       • <number_of_WARN_or_ERROR_lines> is how many lines from step 1 were written to the digest.  
       • <number_of_checksum_lines>  is how many lines from step 2 were written to the digest.

    6. All directories already exist and are writable by the normal user.  
       Work only with standard Unix tools that would be available in a minimal Debian/Ubuntu environment (grep, egrep, sed, awk, sort, tee, cat, etc.).

    7. Because the researcher is in a hurry, run the two filtering operations for step 1 (one per experiment log) *in parallel* so they work at the same time.  
       You may accomplish this with background jobs (&) or any other shell mechanism, but be sure to wait for them to finish before continuing so the merge and sort use complete data.

    8. Do not alter any other files.  
       When you are done, the two new files must exist exactly as specified so an automated script can compare their byte-for-byte contents.
